Q1: My study routine was honestly pretty scattered. I tried to set aside time on weekends and a couple evenings, but with limited resources at home, I often had to work around when I could get good internet access or find a quiet space. I'm someone who learns best by doing things with my hands, so I'd print materials when I could afford to and work through problems physically. Some weeks I'd manage around 19 hours, other weeks less depending on what else was going on in my life.
Q2: My pattern definitely varied a lot based on deadlines and what was happening at home. I'd love to say I had a consistent schedule, but honestly, I studied when I could rather than when I planned to. When assignments were due, I'd cram more hours in, but during quieter weeks I sometimes let things slide. It wasn't ideal, but it was what I could manage with everything going on.
Q3: Usually it was either a deadline approaching or getting a notification that something new was posted. Sometimes my peer study group would message me saying we needed to check something on the LMS, and that would prompt me to log in. I wasn't someone who just browsed it regularly—I needed a specific reason to go there. Fear of missing something important was honestly a big motivator for me.
Q4: Not really, if I'm being honest. The LMS never felt like a natural part of my day. There were maybe a few weeks mid-semester when I got into a rhythm of checking it every couple days, but that didn't last. It always felt more like a chore I had to remember to do rather than something automatic. My peer group helped remind me to stay on top of things, which I really appreciated.
Q5: The video lectures were actually really helpful because I could pause them and work through examples at my own pace. I watched quite a bit of video content because I could replay the parts I didn't understand the first time. The discussion forums were useful too, especially when other students asked questions I hadn't thought of. Seeing how my peers approached problems helped me understand things better than just reading would have.
Q6: I often skipped the supplementary readings and some of the optional practice quizzes. With limited time and honestly limited motivation, I had to prioritize what would directly help my grades. The extra resources felt overwhelming sometimes, and I just couldn't justify spending time on things that weren't going to be assessed. I know that probably hurt my deeper understanding, but I was just trying to get through.
Q7: An activity felt worth my time if I could see a direct connection to assignments or exams. If I could actually do something with my hands—work through a problem, practice a skill—that made it feel valuable. Activities that were just passive reading or watching without any application felt less worthwhile. I also valued things my peer group recommended because I trusted their judgment about what mattered.
Q8: Optional activities felt like luxuries I couldn't afford, honestly. Required work had clear consequences, so I prioritized that. Optional stuff always got pushed to 'later' which usually meant never. There was always this guilty feeling about not doing them, but when you're stretched thin, you have to make choices. The required work felt urgent while optional work felt like it could wait indefinitely.
Q9: Yes, definitely. I downloaded lecture slides and worked through them offline a lot because my internet isn't always reliable. I'd print problem sets when I could and work through them by hand—that's how I learn best anyway. I also had a study group that met in person, and we'd work through concepts together using our notes and whatever materials we'd saved. A lot of my real learning happened away from the computer.
Q10: There were many times when I was actively studying but it wouldn't show online. Like when my peer group and I would meet at someone's apartment and work through problems on paper together. Or when I'd be on the bus reviewing printed notes. I'd sometimes spend hours with downloaded materials, working through examples physically, writing things out. The LMS would just show I downloaded something once, not all the time I spent with it after.
Q11: All the time, actually. I'd download videos when I had good internet and watch them later when I didn't. Same with PDFs and lecture slides—I'd grab them in one session and then study from them for days. My internet situation kind of forced me into this pattern, but honestly it worked better for my learning style anyway. I could annotate printed materials and spread things out physically to see connections.
Q12: There were definitely hard moments, especially mid-semester when multiple deadlines piled up. I remember feeling really scared about falling behind—that fear was constant, actually. When I was anxious about whether I was doing enough, it paradoxically made it harder to engage because I'd avoid logging in to see what I was missing. Financial stress also affected my focus; it's hard to concentrate on coursework when you're worried about other things.
Q13: Absolutely, all of these affected me. When I was tired from everything else in my life, the LMS felt like this overwhelming thing I didn't want to face. Confusion about what was expected made me procrastinate because I didn't know where to start. Competing responsibilities—work, family stuff—meant the LMS often got pushed aside. I'd tell myself I'd catch up later, but later kept getting further away.
Q14: Yes, there were times I'd have the LMS open but I wasn't really absorbing anything. I'd be scrolling through materials while my mind was somewhere else, worrying about other things. Sometimes I'd watch a video but realize at the end I hadn't retained any of it because I was too anxious. It looked like engagement on the surface, but my brain wasn't really there. Those sessions felt like wasted time.
Q15: I definitely avoided things, especially when I was scared I wouldn't understand them or would do poorly. The fear of confirming that I was struggling made me put off looking at feedback or starting difficult assignments. I knew it was making things worse, but the anxiety was hard to push through. My peer group helped with this—sometimes I needed someone else to face the scary stuff with me.
Q16: Deadlines basically controlled everything about how I used the LMS. I'd have these bursts of activity right before things were due, then go quiet for a while. It wasn't healthy, but the pressure of a deadline was sometimes the only thing that could overcome my avoidance. Without a deadline, I'd convince myself I had more time than I did. The deadline was like an external force that made me act despite my fear.
Q17: Feedback was complicated for me. Good feedback motivated me to keep going, but I was often scared to look at feedback because I feared it would confirm I was failing. Sometimes I'd delay checking grades for days because of the anxiety. When I did get encouraging feedback, it really helped my mood and made me want to engage more. The peer support intervention actually helped me process feedback better because I wasn't facing it alone.
Q18: The course structure felt a bit overwhelming with so many online components. Having ten different courses to juggle meant I couldn't give this one as much attention as it probably needed. The weekly modules gave some structure, but I often fell behind and then felt too scared to catch up. A more flexible structure might have helped, or maybe clearer priorities about what was essential versus optional.
Q19: The video content was good for my learning style because I could pause and practice along. But the sheer amount of material made engagement harder—I never felt like I was on top of everything. Having my peer group helped a lot; that human connection made the course feel less isolating. The discussion forums were helpful when active, but sometimes they felt like just another thing to keep up with.
Q20: I think that could happen when someone is anxious and checking things repeatedly without actually making progress. Like refreshing to see if grades are posted, or re-reading instructions because you're scared you missed something. It could also be someone who's lost and clicking around trying to figure out what they're supposed to do. High activity doesn't always mean productive activity—sometimes it's just anxiety in action.
Q21: That actually resonates with me somewhat. I did a lot of studying offline, so my LMS activity was lower than my actual engagement with the material. My exam score was decent even though my online activity wasn't super high. I think students who download materials and study independently might look disengaged online but actually be learning effectively. The LMS only sees one piece of the picture.
Q22: Course logs can show patterns like when you're active and what you click on, but they miss the quality of that engagement. They can't tell if you're actually understanding the material or just going through motions. They definitely miss offline studying, group work, and the emotional side of learning. They might show I watched a video, but not that I paused it fifty times to work through problems by hand.
Q23: Being engaged means actually caring about understanding the material, not just completing tasks. It's when you're curious and want to figure things out, when you think about course concepts outside of study time. For me, it also means connecting with peers and feeling like you're part of a learning community. It's more than just logging in—it's being mentally and emotionally invested in learning.
Q24: The LMS wouldn't show how scared I felt most of the semester, or how much my peer group helped me push through that fear. It wouldn't capture the hours I spent studying from printed materials or working through problems with friends in person. It also wouldn't show how my mood improved after connecting with peers, or how financial stress affected my ability to focus. The human side of my experience is completely invisible in the data.
